_doc: Standard solutions / next steps / additional logs per problem_type. Keyed by the same problem_type strings declared in registry.json. Skills consume this via _shared/playbook.py.

solutions.title: Restart the application pool serving the affected site
steps: Identify the pool: Get-IISAppPool | Where-Object State -eq Started
Recycle: Restart-WebAppPool -Name <pool>
Re-check failure rate via iis_logs --report

solutions.title: Cap concurrent requests / queue length to prevent thread starvation
steps: Edit applicationHost.config: <applicationPools>/<add>/<processModel maxProcesses, queueLength>
Tune <serverRuntime appConcurrentRequestLimit> if 503 'Service Unavailable' dominates

next_steps.action: Correlate 5xx burst window with Application event log
Inspect HTTPERR for connection-abandoned reasons
Check for .NET runtime crashes in the same window

additional_logs_needed.why: Need Application/System events to distinguish app vs infra failures
additional_logs_needed.how_to_collect: wevtutil epl Application app.evtx; wevtutil epl System sys.evtx

additional_logs_needed.why: Reveals server-side disconnect reasons not visible in W3C logs

solutions.title: Identify the slowest endpoint and add caching or async handling
steps: Sort iis_logs report by p95 time-taken per cs-uri-stem
For data-bound endpoints add output-cache; for upstream calls switch to async I/O

solutions.title: Check host resource saturation around the slow window
steps: Run resource_monitor against the same time_range
If CPU > 85% sustained, scale out or tune compression/SSL offload

next_steps.action: Sample perf counters during the slow window
Cross-check Application event log for GC / pool recycle events

solutions.title: Verify the auth provider (Windows / Forms / Bearer) matches the request
steps: Inspect web.config <authentication>; ensure modules are loaded
Test with a known-good principal to isolate config vs. credential issue

solutions.title: If 401 spikes from few IPs, treat as credential stuffing — apply lockout
steps: Block source IPs at firewall (see firewall skill)
Enable Account Lockout policy via secpol.msc
Audit Security event log for 4625 events

next_steps.action: Audit Security event log for 4625 / 4740
Correlate failing principals across hosts

additional_logs_needed.why: 401 alone does not show which principal failed

solutions.title: Drop or rate-limit the offending source ranges at the perimeter
steps: Use firewall skill recommendations to draft block rules
Review WAF / reverse proxy logs to confirm no legitimate clients in the same range

next_steps.action: Pull Windows Firewall log for the same window

additional_logs_needed.why: Confirm whether requests were already dropped at TCP layer

solutions.title: Audit broken links / missing static assets
steps: Group the 404 URIs and verify the file/route should exist
If a deploy removed files, restore them or update referring pages

solutions.title: Verify capacity headroom for the observed peak rate
steps: Compute peak RPS from iis_logs metrics
If single-host > 70% CPU at peak, plan horizontal scale-out

next_steps.action: Confirm resource utilisation during the peak

solutions.title: Tighten FTP authentication and disable Basic over plaintext
steps: In IIS Manager > FTP Authentication, disable Anonymous if not required
Require FTPS (Explicit TLS) and reject plaintext logons

next_steps.action: Look for matching firewall drops or admits
Check Security log for 4625 events from the same source

additional_logs_needed.why: Confirm source IPs are not already blocked

solutions.title: Check disk space and FTP user write permissions
steps: Get-PSDrive C; verify free space
Verify NTFS ACLs on the upload root for the FTP user

next_steps.action: Inspect Application/System events around the failure window

solutions.title: Block the attacking IP range and enable connection throttling
steps: Add a Windows Firewall block rule for the offending /24 or /16
Enable FTP IP Restrictions: set 'Allow only specified clients'
Force account lockout after N failures via Local Security Policy

next_steps.action: Cross-reference dropped packets in firewall log

additional_logs_needed.why: Confirm whether the firewall already mitigates the brute-force

solutions.title: Investigate client disconnects (network jitter, idle timeout)
steps: Check FTP service idle-timeout (default 120s)
Increase if legitimate large uploads time out

next_steps.action: Look for SCHANNEL / TCP/IP events in System log

solutions.title: Compare client-side net-export with IIS access log
steps: Reproduce the request with edge://net-export active
Correlate the netlog URL_REQUEST entry against the IIS W3C row for the same timestamp
If IIS never received the request, treat as a network / proxy / TLS issue

next_steps.action: Replay the request with curl --trace to isolate the failure layer

additional_logs_needed.why: Client-side view is required to determine whether the request ever reached IIS.
additional_logs_needed.how_to_collect: Open edge://net-export, click Start Logging To Disk, reproduce the failure, then Stop.

solutions.title: Verify the certificate chain served by the IIS binding
steps: openssl s_client -connect <host>:443 -servername <host>
Check the binding: Get-WebBinding -Name <site> | Select-Object protocol, bindingInformation, certificateHash

next_steps.action: Confirm the failing intermediate from the netlog SSL events

additional_logs_needed.why: Reveals the exact TLS handshake / cert-validation failure the client saw.
additional_logs_needed.how_to_collect: Open edge://net-export, click Start Logging To Disk, reproduce the failure, then Stop.

solutions.title: Check name resolution from the client and from the IIS host
steps: Resolve-DnsName <host> -Type A on both sides
Compare answers against the DNS server expected by policy

next_steps.action: Inspect the HOST_RESOLVER_IMPL_JOB events in the netlog

additional_logs_needed.why: Shows which resolver attempts failed and with which error code.
additional_logs_needed.how_to_collect: Open edge://net-export, click Start Logging To Disk, reproduce the failure, then Stop.

solutions.title: Bypass / fix the proxy configuration for the affected host
steps: Inspect the effective proxy: netsh winhttp show proxy
Test the request with the proxy disabled or with an explicit PAC entry

next_steps.action: Read the PROXY_RESOLUTION_SERVICE events in the netlog

additional_logs_needed.why: Identifies which proxy decision blocked the request and at what hop.
additional_logs_needed.how_to_collect: Open edge://net-export, click Start Logging To Disk, reproduce the failure, then Stop.

solutions.title: Register the correct HTTP SPN against the IIS app-pool identity
steps: Identify the failing SPN in the netlog (AUTH_LIBRARY_INIT_SEC_CTX `spn` param)
List current SPNs: setspn -L <DOMAIN>\<AppPoolSvcAccount>
Add the public host: setspn -S HTTP/<public-host> <DOMAIN>\<AppPoolSvcAccount>
Add the backend host if Edge canonicalises via CNAME: setspn -S HTTP/<backend-host> <DOMAIN>\<AppPoolSvcAccount>
Check for duplicates across the forest: setspn -X
On clients, purge the bad ticket: klist purge; reload to confirm 200 in the netlog

solutions.title: Make IIS use the app-pool identity for Kerberos decryption (useAppPoolCredentials)
steps: In applicationHost.config under <windowsAuthentication useKernelMode="true">, set useAppPoolCredentials="true"
Confirm the app pool runs as the same domain account that owns the SPN
Recycle the app pool and re-test

next_steps.action: Run `setspn -X` on a domain controller to find duplicate HTTP SPNs
Capture a fresh netlog after the SPN change to confirm SEC_E_WRONG_PRINCIPAL is gone

additional_logs_needed.why: Kerberos failures surface as Event 4 (Kerberos client) and 4769 (KDC).
additional_logs_needed.how_to_collect: wevtutil epl Security sec.evtx; on DC also wevtutil epl Microsoft-Windows-Kerberos/Operational kerb.evtx

solutions.title: Trust the site so the browser sends default credentials silently
steps: Add the site origin to the Local Intranet zone (Group Policy: User Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Internet Explorer > Internet Control Panel > Security Page > Site to Zone Assignment List)
For Edge specifically, set the policy AuthServerAllowlist=*.your-domain.tld (and AuthNegotiateDelegateAllowlist if delegation is required)
Verify with `gpresult /h` and re-test; expect the second leg to carry Authorization: Negotiate/NTLM

solutions.title: Re-check IIS Windows Authentication providers and Extended Protection
steps: Confirm WindowsAuthentication module is enabled and Anonymous is disabled on the affected path
If clients are behind an SSL terminator, set <extendedProtection tokenChecking="None"/>
Ensure providers order is `Negotiate,NTLM` for broadest client support

next_steps.action: Capture a netlog with edge://net-export and look for repeated 401 with no Authorization header on the second leg

additional_logs_needed.why: Confirms whether the browser ever attempts the second leg of the handshake.
additional_logs_needed.how_to_collect: Open edge://net-export, Start Logging To Disk, reproduce, Stop.

solutions.title: Diagnose TLS protocol / cipher mismatch on the IIS binding
steps: openssl s_client -connect <host>:443 -servername <host> -tls1_2
Inspect SCHANNEL events 36874 / 36888 in System log
On Windows Server, verify TLS 1.2/1.3 enabled in HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\SCHANNEL\Protocols

next_steps.action: Cross-check System log for SCHANNEL events in the same window

additional_logs_needed.why: Shows the exact SSL_HANDSHAKE_ERROR / alert code the client received.
additional_logs_needed.how_to_collect: Open edge://net-export, Start Logging To Disk, reproduce, Stop.
